informal: Prove that for all $n>1$ that $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ is not a group under multiplication of residue classes.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_1_1_5_corrected (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 < n) :
  ¬ (∀ x : ZMod n, ∃ y : ZMod n, x * y = (1 : ZMod n) ∧ y * x = 1)
reasoning: The informal claim is that (ℤ/nℤ, ·) fails to be a group when n>1. Since multiplication on ZMod n is always associative, closed, and has identity 1, the only group axiom that can fail is the existence of inverses. The Lean statement asserts ¬(∀ x, ∃ y, x·y = 1 ∧ y·x = 1), i.e. not every element has a two-sided multiplicative inverse, which is exactly the failure of the only non-automatic group axiom. Hence "not a group under multiplication" and the Lean negation-of-inverses statement are logically equivalent for ZMod n. The hypothesis 1 < n matches the informal n > 1. So the formalization is strictly faithful.
